title: Meet Your Brain
brainConcept: Your brain can grow and change when you practice (neuroplasticity).
objective: I can point to where my brain works and say that my brain gets stronger when I practice.
materials: None (optional: make a fist to model the brain).

flow.connect: Welcome the group. Everyone wiggles fingers and taps the top of their head: 'That's where your amazing brain lives!' Smile and say we are starting Brain Lab, where we learn how our brain helps us.
flow.teach: Make a fist: thumb tucked inside = the 'feeling part' (downstairs), fingers over it = the 'thinking part' (upstairs). When we are calm, the upstairs helps us think. Big news: your brain grows new connections every time you try something hard. Mistakes help it grow!
flow.practice: Practice a brand-new skill together (snap, wink, or pat-pat-clap pattern). Try it, miss it, laugh, try again. Say together: 'My brain is growing right now!'
flow.close: Each child shows the brain fist and says one word for how their brain feels today (sleepy, happy, ready).

contentQuestions.text: Where in your body is your brain?
What happens to your brain when you practice something new?
How did it feel when you missed the new move and tried again?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is our name for the part of the brain that grows when we practice?
What is one thing you practiced this week to make your brain stronger?
Show me the brain fist and tell me which part is thinking and which is feeling.

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child learned that the brain gets stronger every time they practice something hard — that's called rewiring. They also found where the brain lives and met its thinking part and its feeling part.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a aprendió que el cerebro se hace más fuerte cada vez que practica algo difícil — eso se llama reconexión. También descubrió dónde vive el cerebro y conoció su parte que piensa y su parte que siente.

askYourChild.en: Where in your body does your brain live?
askYourChild.es: ¿En qué parte de tu cuerpo vive tu cerebro?

askYourChild.en: What happens to your brain when you practice something new?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué le pasa a tu cerebro cuando practicas algo nuevo?

askYourChild.en: How did you feel when you missed the new move and tried again?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cómo te sentiste cuando fallaste el movimiento nuevo y lo intentaste otra vez?

whyThisWorks.en: When children practice something hard, the brain builds new connections and grows — scientists call this neuroplasticity, and it means mistakes are part of getting stronger.
whyThisWorks.es: Cuando los niños practican algo difícil, el cerebro crea nuevas conexiones y crece — los científicos lo llaman neuroplasticidad, y significa que los errores son parte de hacerse más fuerte.

tryTogether.en: Pick one new little skill to practice together this week (a snap, a wink, or a clap pattern). When it feels hard, say together: 'My brain is growing right now!'
tryTogether.es: Elijan una pequeña habilidad nueva para practicar juntos esta semana (chasquear los dedos, guiñar un ojo o un ritmo de palmas). Cuando se sienta difícil, digan juntos: '¡Mi cerebro está creciendo ahora mismo!'

intro.en: Brain Lab — Meet Your Brain. Draw and tell what your brain learned. Bring it back next time!
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Conoce tu Cerebro. Dibuja y cuenta lo que aprendió tu cerebro. ¡Tráelo la próxima vez!

text.en: Draw a picture of where your brain lives in your body.
text.es: Dibuja dónde vive tu cerebro en tu cuerpo.

text.en: Draw your brain getting stronger when you practice something new.
text.es: Dibuja tu cerebro haciéndose más fuerte cuando practicas algo nuevo.

text.en: Name one thing you practiced this week to make your brain stronger.
text.es: Nombra una cosa que practicaste esta semana para hacer tu cerebro más fuerte.

title: Spot Your Signals
brainConcept: Attention is a spotlight you can aim, and your body sends signals about feelings.
objective: I can name a feeling and one thing my body does when I feel it.

flow.connect: Echo: 'Last time we learned our brain can ___ (grow)!' Then play 10 seconds of Spotlight: everyone aims their eyes and points at one object you name.
flow.teach: Your brain has a spotlight that points at what you care about. It also reads your body: a happy belly, hot cheeks when mad, wobbly tummy when nervous. Those are signals telling you how you feel.
flow.practice: Name a feeling (happy, mad, scared, excited). Kids show it with their face and point to where they feel it in their body. Repeat for 3 feelings.
flow.close: Each child says: 'Right now I feel ___ and my body feels it in my ___.'

contentQuestions.text: What does your body do when you feel happy? When you feel mad?
Where do you feel butterflies when you are nervous?
How can your body help you know how you feel?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what do we call the part of your brain that points at what you care about?
Tell me about a time your body gave you a feeling signal this week.
What feeling is the easiest for you to spot in your body?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced aiming their attention 'spotlight' and learned that the body sends signals about feelings — like a happy belly or hot cheeks when mad.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó cómo apuntar el 'reflector' de su atención y aprendió que el cuerpo envía señales sobre los sentimientos — como una panza feliz o las mejillas calientes cuando se enoja.

askYourChild.en: What does your body do when you feel happy? What about when you feel mad?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué hace tu cuerpo cuando te sientes feliz? ¿Y cuando te sientes enojado/a?

askYourChild.en: Where do you feel butterflies when you are nervous?
askYourChild.es: ¿Dónde sientes las mariposas cuando estás nervioso/a?

askYourChild.en: How can your body help you know how you feel?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cómo te puede ayudar tu cuerpo a saber cómo te sientes?

whyThisWorks.en: Attention works like a spotlight your child can aim on purpose, and naming what the body feels helps the brain understand emotions instead of being surprised by them.
whyThisWorks.es: La atención funciona como un reflector que su hijo/a puede apuntar a propósito, y nombrar lo que siente el cuerpo ayuda al cerebro a entender las emociones en lugar de ser sorprendido por ellas.

tryTogether.en: At dinner, each person names one feeling they had today and points to where they felt it in their body.
tryTogether.es: En la cena, cada persona nombra un sentimiento que tuvo hoy y señala dónde lo sintió en su cuerpo.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Spot Your Signals. Draw and tell how your body shows feelings.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Descubre tus Señales. Dibuja y cuenta cómo tu cuerpo muestra los sentimientos.

text.en: Draw your face and body when you feel happy and when you feel mad.
text.es: Dibuja tu cara y tu cuerpo cuando te sientes feliz y cuando te sientes enojado/a.

text.en: Draw where you feel butterflies in your body when you are nervous.
text.es: Dibuja dónde sientes las mariposas en tu cuerpo cuando estás nervioso/a.

text.en: Tell about one time your body gave you a feeling signal this week.
text.es: Cuenta sobre una vez que tu cuerpo te dio una señal de un sentimiento esta semana.

title: Catch the Wave
brainConcept: Naming a big feeling helps the calm-down part of the brain take over.
objective: I can use one calm-down tool when a big feeling starts.

flow.connect: Echo: kids name one feeling and where they feel it. Then everyone makes a slow 'wave' with their hand rising up and coming back down.
flow.teach: Big feelings rise like a wave. If we notice early (using yesterday's body signals), we can ride the wave instead of getting knocked over. A great tool: name it. Saying 'I feel mad' helps the thinking brain come back.
flow.practice: Teach 'flower and candle' breathing: smell the flower (breathe in), blow the candle (breathe out), 4 times slowly. Then practice: 'When I feel big, I say my feeling and I breathe.'
flow.close: Each child picks: flower-candle breathing OR naming the feeling, and says when they will use it.

contentQuestions.text: What does it feel like in your body right before a big feeling?
Why is it easier to calm down when you catch the wave early?
Which calm-down tool feels best to you?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is one calm-down tool we practiced?
Tell me about a time you used your tool this week. What happened?
Where is a good spot at school to take your flower-candle breaths?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child learned that big feelings rise like a wave, and that naming the feeling and breathing helps the calm part of the brain take over. Practicing these tools rewires the brain to calm down faster.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a aprendió que los sentimientos grandes suben como una ola, y que nombrar el sentimiento y respirar ayuda a que la parte calmada del cerebro tome el control. Practicar estas herramientas reconecta el cerebro para calmarse más rápido.

askYourChild.en: What does your body feel like right before a big feeling?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué siente tu cuerpo justo antes de un sentimiento grande?

askYourChild.en: What is one calm-down tool you practiced?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuál es una herramienta para calmarte que practicaste?

askYourChild.en: Tell me about a time you used your tool this week.
askYourChild.es: Cuéntame de una vez que usaste tu herramienta esta semana.

whyThisWorks.en: Each time your child practices naming a feeling and breathing, the brain strengthens the calm-down pathway, so over time it becomes easier and more automatic.
whyThisWorks.es: Cada vez que su hijo/a practica nombrar un sentimiento y respirar, el cerebro fortalece el camino para calmarse, así que con el tiempo se vuelve más fácil y automático.

tryTogether.en: Practice 'flower and candle' breathing together: smell the flower (breathe in), blow the candle (breathe out), four slow times before bed.
tryTogether.es: Practiquen juntos la respiración de 'flor y vela': huelan la flor (inhalen), soplen la vela (exhalen), cuatro veces despacio antes de dormir.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Catch the Wave. Draw and show how you calm a big feeling.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Atrapa la Ola. Dibuja y muestra cómo calmas un sentimiento grande.

text.en: Draw a wave to show how a big feeling rises and comes back down.
text.es: Dibuja una ola para mostrar cómo un sentimiento grande sube y vuelve a bajar.

text.en: Draw or show one calm-down tool you can use.
text.es: Dibuja o muestra una herramienta para calmarte que puedes usar.

text.en: Tell about a time you used your tool this week and what happened.
text.es: Cuenta de una vez que usaste tu herramienta esta semana y qué pasó.

title: Aim Your Spotlight
brainConcept: You can move your attention spotlight on purpose, even when something is distracting.
objective: I can put my attention spotlight on one thing and bring it back when it wanders.
materials: None (optional: a small object to focus on).

flow.connect: Echo: everyone shows a calm-down tool from last time. Then a 10-second 'spotlight on my hands' freeze.
flow.teach: Your spotlight wanders — that's normal! The skill is noticing it wandered and bringing it back, like a puppy on a leash. Every time you bring it back, your brain gets stronger at focus.
flow.practice: Play 'Spotlight Coach': stare at one object for 20 seconds. When your mind wanders, whisper 'come back' and look again. Do it twice.
flow.close: Each child says one place at school they need their spotlight (carpet time, lining up, reading).

contentQuestions.text: What pulls your spotlight away when you are trying to focus?
What can you say to yourself to bring your spotlight back?
When is focusing hardest for you?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what do we do when our spotlight wanders?
Tell me about a time you brought your spotlight back this week.
What is one trick that helps you focus?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced aiming their attention 'spotlight' on one thing and gently bringing it back when it wanders — a key focus skill.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó apuntar el 'reflector' de su atención a una cosa y traerlo de vuelta con calma cuando se distrae — una habilidad clave para concentrarse.

askYourChild.en: What pulls your spotlight away when you are trying to focus?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué jala tu reflector cuando tratas de concentrarte?

askYourChild.en: What can you say to yourself to bring your spotlight back?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué te puedes decir a ti mismo/a para traer tu reflector de vuelta?

askYourChild.en: When is focusing the hardest for you?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuándo te cuesta más concentrarte?

whyThisWorks.en: Attention naturally wanders; the focus muscle grows every time your child notices it drifted and chooses to bring it back, so the noticing is the win.
whyThisWorks.es: La atención se distrae por naturaleza; el músculo de la concentración crece cada vez que su hijo/a nota que se distrajo y decide volver, así que darse cuenta ya es un triunfo.

tryTogether.en: Play a 20-second 'spotlight' game: stare at one object together, and whisper 'come back' when your mind wanders.
tryTogether.es: Jueguen un juego de 'reflector' de 20 segundos: miren juntos un objeto y susurren 'regresa' cuando la mente se distraiga.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Aim Your Spotlight. Draw and tell how you bring your focus back.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Apunta tu Reflector. Dibuja y cuenta cómo traes tu concentración de vuelta.

text.en: Draw something that pulls your spotlight away when you try to focus.
text.es: Dibuja algo que jala tu reflector cuando tratas de concentrarte.

text.en: Write or say the words you use to bring your spotlight back.
text.es: Escribe o di las palabras que usas para traer tu reflector de vuelta.

text.en: Tell about a time you brought your spotlight back this week.
text.es: Cuenta de una vez que trajiste tu reflector de vuelta esta semana.

title: Read the Room
brainConcept: Your brain reads faces and bodies to guess how others feel.
objective: I can look at a face or body and guess how someone might feel.

flow.connect: Echo: kids show where they aim their spotlight to focus. Then everyone makes a 'happy face' and a 'sad face.'
flow.teach: Your brain is a feelings-detective. It reads faces, voices, and bodies to guess how someone feels. Slumped shoulders might mean sad; big smile might mean happy.
flow.practice: Leader acts out a feeling with face and body (no words). Kids guess and point to the clue that helped them (mouth, eyes, shoulders). Do 3 rounds.
flow.close: Each child names one clue they will look for to read how a friend feels.

contentQuestions.text: What clues tell you someone is sad? Happy? Mad?
Can two people feel different things at the same time?
How does it help to know how a friend feels?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name one face clue that shows a feeling.
Tell me about a time you noticed how a friend felt this week.
Who is someone whose feelings you are good at reading?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced being a 'feelings detective' — using their attention spotlight to read faces, voices, and bodies to guess how others feel.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó ser un 'detective de sentimientos' — usando el reflector de su atención para leer caras, voces y cuerpos y adivinar cómo se sienten los demás.

askYourChild.en: What clues tell you someone is sad? Happy? Mad?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué pistas te dicen que alguien está triste? ¿Feliz? ¿Enojado?

askYourChild.en: Can two people feel different things at the same time?
askYourChild.es: ¿Pueden dos personas sentir cosas diferentes al mismo tiempo?

askYourChild.en: Tell me about a time you noticed how a friend felt this week.
askYourChild.es: Cuéntame de una vez que notaste cómo se sentía un amigo/a esta semana.

whyThisWorks.en: When your child aims their attention on a person's face and body, the brain gathers clues to understand others — a skill that grows stronger with practice.
whyThisWorks.es: Cuando su hijo/a apunta su atención a la cara y el cuerpo de una persona, el cerebro junta pistas para entender a los demás — una habilidad que se fortalece con la práctica.

tryTogether.en: Take turns making a feeling face with no words and guessing it — then point to the clue (eyes, mouth, shoulders) that helped.
tryTogether.es: Túrnense para hacer una cara de un sentimiento sin palabras y adivinarla — luego señalen la pista (ojos, boca, hombros) que ayudó.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Read the Room. Draw and tell how faces show feelings.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Lee el Ambiente. Dibuja y cuenta cómo las caras muestran sentimientos.

text.en: Draw a happy face, a sad face, and a mad face. Show the clues.
text.es: Dibuja una cara feliz, una triste y una enojada. Muestra las pistas.

text.en: Name one face clue that shows how someone feels.
text.es: Nombra una pista de la cara que muestra cómo se siente alguien.

text.en: Tell about a time you noticed how a friend felt this week.
text.es: Cuenta de una vez que notaste cómo se sentía un amigo/a esta semana.

title: Walk in Their Shoes
brainConcept: Your brain can imagine what someone else is thinking or wanting.
objective: I can guess what someone else might want or feel in a situation.

flow.connect: Echo: kids name a feeling clue from last time. Then everyone imagines: 'Pretend you dropped your ice cream — show your face.'
flow.teach: Your brain can pretend to be in someone else's shoes. When we imagine how they feel, we connect it to a time we felt that way (that's the Velcro — sticking new to what we know).
flow.practice: Tell a tiny story (a new kid stands alone at recess). Kids answer: How might the new kid feel? What might help? Connect to: 'When did you feel left out?'
flow.close: Each child finishes: 'I can help someone feel better by ___.'

contentQuestions.text: How might the new kid in the story feel?
When have you felt the same way?
What is one kind thing that would help?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what does it mean to walk in someone's shoes?
Tell me about a time you helped someone feel included this week.
Who could you invite to play tomorrow?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced imagining how someone else feels by connecting it to a time they felt the same way — that's the brain's 'Velcro,' sticking new ideas to what we already know.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó imaginar cómo se siente otra persona conectándolo con una vez que él/ella se sintió igual — ese es el 'Velcro' del cerebro, que pega ideas nuevas a lo que ya sabemos.

askYourChild.en: How might a new kid standing alone at recess feel?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cómo se podría sentir un niño/a nuevo/a que está solo/a en el recreo?

askYourChild.en: When have you felt the same way?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuándo te has sentido de la misma manera?

askYourChild.en: What is one kind thing that would help?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué es una cosa amable que ayudaría?

whyThisWorks.en: The brain understands a new feeling best by sticking it to a memory of a time your child felt the same — connecting new to known makes the lesson stick (Velcro).
whyThisWorks.es: El cerebro entiende mejor un sentimiento nuevo cuando lo pega a un recuerdo de cuando su hijo/a se sintió igual — conectar lo nuevo con lo conocido hace que la lección se quede (Velcro).

tryTogether.en: When you read a story or watch a show, pause and ask: 'How does that character feel? When did you feel like that?'
tryTogether.es: Cuando lean un cuento o vean un programa, hagan una pausa y pregunten: '¿Cómo se siente ese personaje? ¿Cuándo te sentiste así?'

intro.en: Brain Lab — Walk in Their Shoes. Draw and tell how you help a friend feel included.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Ponte en sus Zapatos. Dibuja y cuenta cómo ayudas a un amigo/a a sentirse incluido/a.

text.en: Draw the new kid at recess and how they might feel.
text.es: Dibuja al niño/a nuevo/a en el recreo y cómo se podría sentir.

text.en: Write one kind thing you could do to help.
text.es: Escribe una cosa amable que podrías hacer para ayudar.

text.en: Tell about a time you helped someone feel included this week.
text.es: Cuenta de una vez que ayudaste a alguien a sentirse incluido/a esta semana.

title: Say It So They Hear It
brainConcept: Words stick better when they connect to what the listener already knows (Velcro).
objective: I can use my words to tell someone what I need in a kind way.

flow.connect: Echo: kids share one kind thing they did. Then practice a calm 'inside voice' all together.
flow.teach: When we want something, our words work better than grabbing or yelling. A simple shape: 'I feel ___, can you ___?' Calm words help the other person's brain listen.
flow.practice: Role-play: someone took your crayon. Practice: 'I feel sad, can I have it back please?' Each child tries with a partner.
flow.close: Each child shares one 'I feel ___, can you ___?' they could use.

contentQuestions.text: What happens when we grab or yell instead of using words?
Why do calm words help someone listen?
What is a kind way to ask for something back?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what are the two parts of our asking shape?
Tell me about a time you used your words this week.
What is something you might need to ask for tomorrow?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced using kind words to ask for what they need with a simple shape: 'I feel ___, can you ___?' Calm words help the other person's brain listen.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó usar palabras amables para pedir lo que necesita con una frase sencilla: 'Me siento ___, ¿puedes ___?' Las palabras calmadas ayudan a que el cerebro de la otra persona escuche.

askYourChild.en: What happens when we grab or yell instead of using words?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué pasa cuando arrebatamos o gritamos en vez de usar palabras?

askYourChild.en: Why do calm words help someone listen?
askYourChild.es: ¿Por qué las palabras calmadas ayudan a que alguien escuche?

askYourChild.en: What are the two parts of our asking shape?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuáles son las dos partes de nuestra frase para pedir?

whyThisWorks.en: Calm, clear words connect to what the listener already understands, so the message sticks — and the listener's brain can stay calm enough to help.
whyThisWorks.es: Las palabras calmadas y claras se conectan con lo que el oyente ya entiende, así el mensaje se queda — y el cerebro del oyente puede mantenerse calmado para ayudar.

tryTogether.en: Practice the shape together: 'I feel ___, can you ___?' Use it next time someone in the family needs something.
tryTogether.es: Practiquen juntos la frase: 'Me siento ___, ¿puedes ___?' Úsenla la próxima vez que alguien en la familia necesite algo.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Say It So They Hear It. Draw and tell how you ask with kind words.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Dilo para que te Escuchen. Dibuja y cuenta cómo pides con palabras amables.

text.en: Write a kind way to ask for something back: 'I feel ___, can you ___?'
text.es: Escribe una manera amable de pedir algo de vuelta: 'Me siento ___, ¿puedes ___?'

text.en: Draw what happens when we grab or yell instead of using words.
text.es: Dibuja qué pasa cuando arrebatamos o gritamos en vez de usar palabras.

text.en: Tell about a time you used your words this week.
text.es: Cuenta de una vez que usaste tus palabras esta semana.

title: Fix-It Talk
brainConcept: Practicing how to make up strengthens the memory so it is easier next time (Echo).
objective: I can say sorry and offer a way to fix a problem with a friend.

flow.connect: Echo: kids show the asking shape from last time. Then everyone practices a friendly 'I'm sorry' voice.
flow.teach: Everyone bumps into problems with friends. A Fix-It Talk has 3 steps: say what happened, say sorry, offer to make it better ('Want to play together?'). Practicing it makes it easy to remember.
flow.practice: Role-play: you knocked over a friend's blocks. Walk through the 3 steps with a partner. Switch roles.
flow.close: Each child names the 3 Fix-It steps on their fingers.

contentQuestions.text: What are the three steps of a Fix-It Talk?
Why does saying sorry help a friendship?
What can you offer to make a problem better?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: say the three Fix-It steps.
Tell me about a problem you fixed with a friend this week.
Who might you need to have a Fix-It Talk with soon?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced a 'Fix-It Talk' to make up with a friend: say what happened, say sorry, and offer to make it better. Practicing the steps helps the brain remember them next time.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó una 'Charla para Arreglar' para reconciliarse con un amigo/a: decir qué pasó, pedir perdón y ofrecer cómo mejorarlo. Practicar los pasos ayuda al cerebro a recordarlos la próxima vez.

askYourChild.en: What are the three steps of a Fix-It Talk?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuáles son los tres pasos de una Charla para Arreglar?

askYourChild.en: Why does saying sorry help a friendship?
askYourChild.es: ¿Por qué pedir perdón ayuda a una amistad?

askYourChild.en: Tell me about a problem you fixed with a friend this week.
askYourChild.es: Cuéntame de un problema que arreglaste con un amigo/a esta semana.

whyThisWorks.en: Saying the steps out loud over and over is retrieval practice — each time your child recalls them, the memory gets stronger and easier to use in the moment.
whyThisWorks.es: Decir los pasos en voz alta una y otra vez es práctica de recuperación — cada vez que su hijo/a los recuerda, la memoria se hace más fuerte y más fácil de usar en el momento.

tryTogether.en: Count the three Fix-It steps on your fingers together: say what happened, say sorry, offer to make it better.
tryTogether.es: Cuenten juntos los tres pasos para arreglar con los dedos: decir qué pasó, pedir perdón, ofrecer cómo mejorarlo.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Fix-It Talk. Draw and tell how you make up with a friend.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Charla para Arreglar. Dibuja y cuenta cómo te reconcilias con un amigo/a.

text.en: Draw the three steps of a Fix-It Talk.
text.es: Dibuja los tres pasos de una Charla para Arreglar.

text.en: Write one thing you can offer to make a problem better.
text.es: Escribe una cosa que puedes ofrecer para mejorar un problema.

text.en: Tell about a problem you fixed with a friend this week.
text.es: Cuenta de un problema que arreglaste con un amigo/a esta semana.

title: Stop & Plan
brainConcept: The thinking brain (upstairs) helps you stop and plan before you act.
objective: I can stop, think, and pick a good choice before I act.

flow.connect: Echo: kids name the Fix-It steps. Then play red light / green light for 30 seconds (practice stopping).
flow.teach: Before we act, our thinking brain can say STOP. Then we plan: 'What could I do? What might happen?' Stopping gives the upstairs brain time to help.
flow.practice: Scenario: you want a turn on the swing but a friend is on it. Stop. Name 2 choices. Pick the kind one. Do a second scenario together.
flow.close: Each child says their STOP word or signal they will use.

contentQuestions.text: Why is it smart to stop before you act?
What are two choices in the swing problem?
Which choice is kind and safe?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what do we do first before making a choice?
Tell me about a time you stopped and planned this week.
What is a STOP signal you can use in class?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced using their thinking brain to STOP and plan before acting — naming two choices and picking the kind, safe one.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó usar su cerebro pensante para PARAR y planear antes de actuar — nombrando dos opciones y eligiendo la que es amable y segura.

askYourChild.en: Why is it smart to stop before you act?
askYourChild.es: ¿Por qué es inteligente parar antes de actuar?

askYourChild.en: If a friend is on the swing you want, what are two choices?
askYourChild.es: Si un amigo/a está en el columpio que tú quieres, ¿cuáles son dos opciones?

askYourChild.en: What STOP signal can you use in class?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué señal de PARAR puedes usar en clase?

whyThisWorks.en: Stopping puts attention back on the thinking part of the brain, giving it the moment it needs to plan a good choice instead of reacting fast.
whyThisWorks.es: Parar vuelve a poner la atención en la parte pensante del cerebro, dándole el momento que necesita para planear una buena decisión en vez de reaccionar rápido.

tryTogether.en: Pick a family STOP signal (a hand up, a word) and use it together when someone feels rushed to react.
tryTogether.es: Elijan una señal de PARAR para la familia (una mano arriba, una palabra) y úsenla juntos cuando alguien sienta prisa por reaccionar.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Stop & Plan. Draw and tell how you stop and pick a good choice.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Para y Planea. Dibuja y cuenta cómo paras y eliges una buena decisión.

text.en: Draw the two choices in the swing problem.
text.es: Dibuja las dos opciones en el problema del columpio.

text.en: Circle or write the choice that is kind and safe.
text.es: Marca o escribe la opción que es amable y segura.

text.en: Draw your STOP signal you will use in class.
text.es: Dibuja tu señal de PARAR que usarás en clase.

title: Weigh It Out
brainConcept: Your brain can imagine what might happen next (mental time travel).
objective: I can think about what might happen after a choice.

flow.connect: Echo: kids share their STOP signal. Then everyone makes a 'thinking face.'
flow.teach: Your brain can peek into the future! Before choosing, ask: 'What might happen if I do this?' A good choice helps you and others. We connect it to times choices went well or not (Velcro).
flow.practice: Scenario: a friend dares you to push someone. Imagine what happens after pushing vs. walking away. Compare the two endings together.
flow.close: Each child finishes: 'A good choice helps ___.'

contentQuestions.text: What might happen if you push someone?
What might happen if you walk away?
Which ending is better and why?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what question do we ask before choosing?
Tell me about a good choice you made this week.
How did you know it was a good choice?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced 'peeking into the future' — imagining what might happen after a choice — and connecting it to times their choices went well or not (Velcro).
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó 'espiar el futuro' — imaginar qué podría pasar después de una decisión — y conectarlo con veces que sus decisiones salieron bien o no (Velcro).

askYourChild.en: What might happen if you push someone? What if you walk away?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué podría pasar si empujas a alguien? ¿Y si te alejas?

askYourChild.en: Which ending is better, and why?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuál final es mejor, y por qué?

askYourChild.en: Tell me about a good choice you made this week.
askYourChild.es: Cuéntame de una buena decisión que tomaste esta semana.

whyThisWorks.en: Imagining what comes next connects a choice to past experiences, helping the brain predict outcomes and pick the choice that helps everyone.
whyThisWorks.es: Imaginar lo que viene después conecta una decisión con experiencias pasadas, ayudando al cerebro a predecir los resultados y elegir la opción que ayuda a todos.

tryTogether.en: Before a small decision this week, ask together: 'What might happen if we do this?'
tryTogether.es: Antes de una pequeña decisión esta semana, pregunten juntos: '¿Qué podría pasar si hacemos esto?'

intro.en: Brain Lab — Weigh It Out. Draw and tell what might happen after a choice.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Piénsalo Bien. Dibuja y cuenta qué podría pasar después de una decisión.

text.en: Draw what might happen if you push someone.
text.es: Dibuja qué podría pasar si empujas a alguien.

text.en: Draw what might happen if you walk away.
text.es: Dibuja qué podría pasar si te alejas.

text.en: Tell about a good choice you made this week.
text.es: Cuenta de una buena decisión que tomaste esta semana.

title: Put It Together
brainConcept: Connecting many small skills builds one strong web in your brain.
objective: I can use more than one Brain Lab tool in a pretend problem.

flow.connect: Echo round: each child shouts out one tool we learned (breathe, name it, spotlight, Fix-It, STOP).
flow.teach: All our tools connect like Velcro into one strong web. In a real problem you might use a few: notice your feeling, breathe, then make a smart choice.
flow.practice: Big scenario: someone cuts in line and you feel mad. Walk through it as a group: spot the feeling, calm down, use words, pick a choice.
flow.close: Each child picks the two tools they like best.

contentQuestions.text: Which tools could help when someone cuts in line?
What would you do first?
Which tool is your favorite?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name three Brain Lab tools.
Tell me about a time you used two tools together this week.
Which tool do you want to practice more?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child practiced using more than one tool together in a pretend problem — noticing a feeling, calming down, and making a smart choice. All the tools connect like Velcro into one strong web.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a practicó usar más de una herramienta junta en un problema de práctica — notar un sentimiento, calmarse y tomar una buena decisión. Todas las herramientas se conectan como Velcro en una red fuerte.

askYourChild.en: Which tools could help when someone cuts in line?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué herramientas podrían ayudar cuando alguien se mete en la fila?

askYourChild.en: What would you do first?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué harías primero?

askYourChild.en: Which tool is your favorite?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuál es tu herramienta favorita?

whyThisWorks.en: Linking the small skills together connects them in the brain like a web, so your child can reach for several tools at once during a real problem.
whyThisWorks.es: Unir las pequeñas habilidades las conecta en el cerebro como una red, así su hijo/a puede usar varias herramientas a la vez durante un problema real.

tryTogether.en: Make up a tiny problem at home and walk through it together: spot the feeling, breathe, use words, pick a choice.
tryTogether.es: Inventen un pequeño problema en casa y resuélvanlo juntos: noten el sentimiento, respiren, usen palabras, elijan una opción.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Put It Together. Draw and tell which tools you use.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Júntalo Todo. Dibuja y cuenta qué herramientas usas.

text.en: Draw the tools you could use when someone cuts in line.
text.es: Dibuja las herramientas que podrías usar cuando alguien se mete en la fila.

text.en: Name three Brain Lab tools you learned.
text.es: Nombra tres herramientas del Laboratorio del Cerebro que aprendiste.

text.en: Tell about a time you used two tools together this week.
text.es: Cuenta de una vez que usaste dos herramientas juntas esta semana.

title: Rewire & Reflect
brainConcept: Practicing again and again over time makes brain connections strong and lasting.
objective: I can name a tool I will keep practicing to make my brain stronger.
materials: None (optional: a small certificate or sticker).

flow.connect: Echo party: go around twice, each child names a tool until we run out.
flow.teach: Your brain got stronger in Brain Lab because you practiced! The secret is to keep going — practice over time makes the connection stick (Rewire). Today we pick one tool to keep.
flow.practice: Each child sets a 'Rewire goal': one tool to use this week and where. Group cheers each goal.
flow.close: Celebrate! Everyone does the brain fist and says: 'My brain is growing!' Hand out certificates if available.

contentQuestions.text: What makes a brain connection strong and lasting?
Which tool helped you the most?
Where will you use your tool this week?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is your Rewire goal?
Tell me about how your brain grew during Brain Lab.
Who can you teach one of your tools to?

summary.en: In Brain Lab this week your child celebrated how their brain got stronger through practice and set a 'Rewire goal' — one tool to keep using. Practicing over time makes brain connections strong and lasting.
summary.es: En el Laboratorio del Cerebro esta semana, su hijo/a celebró cómo su cerebro se hizo más fuerte con la práctica y se puso una 'meta de reconexión' — una herramienta para seguir usando. Practicar con el tiempo hace que las conexiones del cerebro sean fuertes y duraderas.

askYourChild.en: What makes a brain connection strong and lasting?
askYourChild.es: ¿Qué hace que una conexión del cerebro sea fuerte y duradera?

askYourChild.en: Which tool helped you the most?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuál herramienta te ayudó más?

askYourChild.en: What is your Rewire goal for this week?
askYourChild.es: ¿Cuál es tu meta de reconexión para esta semana?

whyThisWorks.en: Brain connections grow strong and lasting only with repeated practice over time — so cheering your child on to keep using a tool is what makes it permanent (neuroplasticity).
whyThisWorks.es: Las conexiones del cerebro se hacen fuertes y duraderas solo con la práctica repetida a lo largo del tiempo — así que animar a su hijo/a a seguir usando una herramienta es lo que la hace permanente (neuroplasticidad).

tryTogether.en: Help your child keep their Rewire goal: pick one tool, name where they'll use it, and cheer each time they do.
tryTogether.es: Ayuden a su hijo/a a cumplir su meta de reconexión: elijan una herramienta, digan dónde la usará y celebren cada vez que lo haga.

intro.en: Brain Lab — Rewire & Reflect. Draw and tell your goal to keep your brain growing.
intro.es: Laboratorio del Cerebro — Reconecta y Reflexiona. Dibuja y cuenta tu meta para seguir haciendo crecer tu cerebro.

text.en: Write or draw your Rewire goal: one tool you will keep practicing.
text.es: Escribe o dibuja tu meta de reconexión: una herramienta que seguirás practicando.

text.en: Which tool helped you the most? Write or draw it.
text.es: ¿Cuál herramienta te ayudó más? Escríbela o dibújala.

text.en: Draw how your brain grew during Brain Lab.
text.es: Dibuja cómo creció tu cerebro durante el Laboratorio del Cerebro.
